items.q: Can very bright light below the threshold frequency release photoelectrons?
items.correct: No — each photon still lacks the energy to overcome the work function, however many arrive.
items.misconception: Yes — sufficient intensity will eventually free electrons.
items.random_wrong: Yes — after about a minute of warming up.
items.why: Wave thinking suggests energy accumulates; the photoelectric effect is a one-photon, one-electron process.

items.q: Is light a wave or a particle?
items.correct: It shows both behaviours — interference reveals its wave nature; the photoelectric effect reveals its particle nature.
items.misconception: It must be one or the other, and experiments will eventually settle which.
items.random_wrong: It is neither — light is a kind of very fast sound.
items.why: Students want a single classical category; duality means the appropriate model depends on the experiment.

items.correct: Yes — electron beams produce diffraction rings, showing matter has a de Broglie wavelength.
items.misconception: No — diffraction is only for waves like light, not for particles.
items.why: Students keep waves and particles in separate boxes, so wave behaviour of matter comes as a surprise.

items.q: Diffraction through a gap is most noticeable when the gap width is:
items.correct: About the same size as the wavelength
items.why: It's About the same size as the wavelength. “Much larger than the wavelength” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which observation supports the particle (photon) model of light?
items.correct: The threshold frequency in the photoelectric effect
items.why: Correct answer: The threshold frequency in the photoelectric effect. “Diffraction through a slit” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Particles can behave as waves.. “Electrons are photons.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Increasing the intensity of light below the threshold frequency:
items.random_wrong: Releases electrons after a short delay.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Still releases no photoelectrons.. “Raises the metal's work function.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A wave completes 20 oscillations in 4 seconds. What is its frequency?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 Hz. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
